coredata.dc:title: A dual-emission carbon dots-based nonenzymatic fluorescent sensing platform for simultaneous detection of parathion-methyl and glyphosate
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Yangyu
Tong, Xia
Chen, Yuxia
Zhou, Rongrong
Cai, Guihan
Wang, Tongtao
Zhang, Shuihan
Shi, Shuyun
Guo, Ying
coredata.dc:description: Simultaneous and high-performance detection of pesticides is still a considerable challenge and urgent need. Herein, a dual-emission carbon dots (CDs)-based nonenzymatic fluorescent sensing platform has been developed, which shows excellent sensitivity and selectivity in simultaneously detecting parathion-methyl (MP) and glyphosate. CDs with emissions at 440 nm (bCDs) and 660 nm (rCDs) were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of mulberry leaves and sodium hydroxide. bCDs response to hydrolyzed MP via inner filter effect, while rCDs sense glyphosate with the aid of Cu2+ by static quenching effect. Excellent linear correlations were found for MP (0.3–65.0 μM) and glyphosate (1.0–110.0 μM) with limits of detection at 0.14 and 0.60 μM. Notably, the presented dual-channel strategy was successfully applied in simultaneously detecting MP and glyphosate in food/herbal samples with acceptable recoveries, good precision, and high selectivity. Moreover, an ORlogicgatewas achieved for estimating food, herbal, or environmental safety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Dual-channel mode
Non-enzyme detection
Parathion-methyl
Glyphosate
Molecular logic gate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the dual-emission CDs
Mechanism of simultaneously sensing parathion-methyl and glyphosate
Optimization and detection of parathion-methyl and glyphosate

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Center on the Microenvironment and Metastasis, Cornell University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the Key Research and Development projects in Hunan Province, China (2021NK2024), the Key Research Program of Hunan Administration of Traditional Chinese Medicine (C2022042), and the Special Project of “National Chinese Materia Medica (CMM) Resources Survey” ([2017] 66).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (inset is HR-TEM) for CDs (A); FT-IR spectrum of CDs (B); XPS survey spectrum of CDs (C); UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs, fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs at 320 nm excitation, and fluorescence excitation spectra of bCDs and rCDs at 440 nm and 660 nm emissions (D).

UV–vis absorption spectra of PNP (35.0 μM) and CDs (225.0 μg/mL) (A); Fluorescence lifetime of bCDs in the absence and presence of PNP (35.0 μM) (B); Fluorescent spectra of rCDs in the presence of Cu2+ (9.0 μM) and Cu2+ (9.0 μM) + glyphosate (100.0 μM) (C); Fluorescence lifetime of rCDs before and after addition of Cu2+ (9.0 μM) (D).

Fluorescence response of bCDs towards MP in PBS solution (10 mM, pH 6.5) (A); Linear fitting curve of (F0 – F)/F at 440 nm versus MP concentration from 0.3 to 65.0 μM (B); The change of fluorescent spectra of rCDs to glyphosate in PBS solution (10 mM, pH 6.5) (C); Linear correlation between (F – F0 )/F0 at 660 nm and glyphosate concentrations (1.0–110.0 μM) (D). The experiments were repeated three times.

(A) Fluorescent spectra of CDs + Cu2+ (9.0 μM)system after addition of different concentrations of hydrolyzed solution containing MP and glyphosate; (B) “OR” logic gate diagram for dual-emission CDs to MP and glyphosate and the truth table of “OR” logic gate.
Schematic representation of dual-emission carbon dots-based nonenzymatic fluorescent sensing strategy for discrimination and simultaneous detection of MP and glyphosate.
Comparison of dual-emission CDs with reported fluorescent sensors for MP and glyphosate assay.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: QDs: quantum dots; UCNPs: upconversion nanoparticles; NPA: N-n-butyl-4- (3-pyridin)ylmethylidenehydrazine-1,8-naphthalimide; BHMH: 2-(benzothiazol)-4- (3-hydroxy-4-methylphenyl) imino phenol.

head.ce:title: A dual-emission carbon dots-based nonenzymatic fluorescent sensing platform for simultaneous detection of parathion-methyl and glyphosate

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Simultaneous and high-performance detection of pesticides is still a considerable challenge and urgent need. Herein, a dual-emission carbon dots (CDs)-based nonenzymatic fluorescent sensing platform has been developed, which shows excellent sensitivity and selectivity in simultaneously detecting parathion-methyl (MP) and glyphosate. CDs with emissions at 440 nm (bCDs) and 660 nm (rCDs) were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of mulberry leaves and sodium hydroxide. bCDs response to hydrolyzed MP via inner filter effect, while rCDs sense glyphosate with the aid of Cu2+ by static quenching effect. Excellent linear correlations were found for MP (0.3–65.0 μM) and glyphosate (1.0–110.0 μM) with limits of detection at 0.14 and 0.60 μM. Notably, the presented dual-channel strategy was successfully applied in simultaneously detecting MP and glyphosate in food/herbal samples with acceptable recoveries, good precision, and high selectivity. Moreover, an ORlogicgatewas achieved for estimating food, herbal, or environmental safety.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Pesticides play significant roles in modern agriculture for their efficient controlling and/or eradicating pests and weeds, and dramatical boosting up the yield of agricultural products (e.g., herbs, crops, vegetables, fruits) (Su, Li, Yan, Lin, & Lu, 2021). Organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) are the mainstay of the pesticide industry, while the excessive use and improper disposal of OPs can bring about unavoidable residues in soil, air, water, and agricultural products (Zhao, Wu, Ying, She, Wang, & Ping, 2018). The long-term uptake of food being contaminated by OPs can result in a variety of chronic complications such as diabetes, neurological disorders, cancer, and asthma (Kim, Kabir, & Arajahan, 2016). Therefore, rapid and sensitive determination of OPs is crucial for evaluating environment, food, and herbal safety, and safeguarding public health (Tong et al., 2022). Gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (Belarbi, Vivier, Zaghouani, Sloovere, Agasse-Peulon, & Cardinael, 2021), liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry (LC–MS) (Almutairi, Alsaleem, Herbish, Sayari, & Alowaifeer, 2021), surface-enhanced Raman scattering (Wang, Sun, Pu, & Wei, 2021), and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (Yin et al., 2021) are widely employed with high sensitivity, acceptable precision, and good accuracy for OPs detection. However, the sophisticated instrumentation, professional operation, and complicated pretreatment and analysis procedures limit their applications in real-time or emergency cases (Su et al., 2021). Thus, facile, reliable, rapid, and straightforward sensing methods for OPs detection are urgent in real sample analysis.

Hitherto, fluorescent methods come out ahead owing to their relatively low cost, facile operation, short assay time, excellent sensitivity, and real-time sensing (Magdy, Hakiem, Belal, & Abdel-Megied, 2021; Mierczynska-Vasilev, Vasilev, Reilly, Bindon, & Vasilev, 2022; Sajwan, & Solanki, 2022). Up to date, different kinds of fluorimetric sensors, such as carbon dots (CDs) (Su et al., 2021), quantum dots (Qiu, Na, Li, Bai, Zhang, & Jin, 2022), metal–organic framework (Luo et al., 2022), and organic dye (Ferri, Gaviña, Costero, Parra, Vivancos, & Martínez-Máñez, 2014), have been fabricated and developed to determine OPs. Among them, CDs have received considerable attentions for their merits on easy preparation, high stability, tunable luminescence, chemical inertness, excellent bio-compatibility, and negligible toxicity (Magdy et al., 2021; Peng, Yang, Zhang, & Jia, 2022; Vijeata, Chaudhary, & Chaudhary, 2022). Development and application of CDs-based fluorescent sensors for pesticides detection have been well reviewed by Su and coworkers, and most sensing strategies are fabricated based on cholinesterase (i.e., acetyl cholinesterase and butyryl cholinesterase) catalytic reaction (Su et al., 2021). In the case of enzymatic platform, OPs can irreversibly inhibit the activity of cholinesterase through nucleophilic reaction with its serine residue (Ser200), then the product thiocholine from substrate acetylcholine is suppressed (Yan et al., 2018). Accordingly, thiocholine is selected as intermedium to bind with metal ions (Ag+, Cu2+), metal nanoparticles (AgNPs, AuNPs), or reduce oxidizing chemicals (MnO2, 5,5′-dithiobis-2-nitrobenoic acid), which can directly or indirectly regulate the fluorescent emission of CDs to quantitatively evaluate OPs (Su et al., 2021; Tong et al., 2022). However, some challenges occur, like (i) broad response to OPs is observed, and OPs can not be discriminated, (ii) enzyme denatures easily, and the activity of enzyme is vulnerable to reaction system (e.g., pH and temperature of solvent) (Zhao et al., 2018). As a result, it is gradually desirable to develop nonenzymatic fluorescent sensing strategies to detect OPs with high specificity, which to our best knowledge is still challenging.

Several kinds of OPs are always sprayed together in practical applications, thus, discrimination and simultaneous detection of two or more OPs are urgently necessary. Parathion-methyl (MP) and glyphosate have been extensively used as insecticide and herbicide (Sun et al., 2021a; Yan, Li, Yan, & Su, 2015). MP, p-nitrophenyl phosphate derivative, can be hydrolyzed by organic phosphorus hydrolase or under alkaline conditions to produce p-nitrophenol (PNP), which can quench the fluorescence of CDs by inner filter effect (IFE) (Lan et al., 2019; Tong, Shi, Tong, Iftikhar, Long, & Zhu, 2020). Glyphosate can coordinate with Cu2+ or Fe3+ (Sun et al., 2021), hence, competitive coordination between glyphosate and CDs can be developed to detect glyphosate (Wang, Bi, Gao, Li, Ding, & Ding, 2016). Obviously, CDs-based nonenzymatic fluorescent sensing strategies can be designed and developed to efficiently detect MP or glyphosate. Regrettably, no reports mentioned CDs-based sensing platform to discriminate and simultaneously detect MP and glyphosate perhaps because of the difficulty in concurrently monitoring two reaction systems.

Dual-emission CDs are always constructed for ratiometric sensing of metal ions or organic small molecules (Han et al., 2022). Notably, biomass-derived dual-emission CDs can be simply prepared by solvothermal method, in which blue emission (bCDs) with excitation-dependent behavior comes from the intrinsic structure, and red emission (rCDs) with excitation-independent behavior originates from surface chlorophyll (Long et al., 2020a; Zhao et al., 2017). Different fluorescent characteristics of biomass-derived dual-emission CDs might response different signals, which might exhibit amazing advantages for dual-channel differentiating two analyzed targets. Our previous reports indicated that dual-emission CDs synthesized from cabbage can be used for dual-channel sensing of pH and Hg2+ (Long et al., 2020b), and kiwifruit derived dual-emission CDs have been designed for dual-channel determination of γ-glutamyl transpeptidase and alkaline phosphatase (Tong, Zhu, Tong, Shi, Long, & Guo 2021). Thus, we speculate that MP and glyphosate can also be efficiently discriminated and sensitively determined using biomass-derived dual-emission CDs after professional design.

In the presented work, dual-emission CDs (Ex, 320 nm; Em for bCDs and rCDs at 440 and 660 nm) were simply synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal treatment of mulberry leaves and sodium hydroxide, which were then applied to develop a new nonenzymatic fluorescent sensing platform for efficient discrimination and detection of MP and glyphosate. As shown in Scheme 1 , PNP, the alkalinehydrolyzedproduct of MP, can quench the emission of bCDs because of the overlaps between absorption spectrum of PNP and excitation and emission spectra of bCDs. As a result, MP can be quantitatively determined by bCDs (Ex 320 nm; Em 440 nm) by IFE mechanism. Surface chlorophyll can selectively coordinate with Cu2+, then emission of rCDs can be efficiently quenched by static quenching effect (SQE). After addition of glyphosate, emission of rCDs was recovered for more powerful chelation between Cu2+ and glyphosate. Hence, glyphosate can be detected using rCDs (Ex 505 nm; Em 660 nm). Here, appropriate selection of excitation wavelengths of bCDs and rCDs is crucial for improvement of accuracy and avoidance of crosstalk for detection of MP and glyphosate in real samples. In particular, dual-emission CDs have been used as signal generators to construct a logic gate system for detection of MP, glyphosate, and their mixtures.
Materials and apparatus were described in Electronic Supplementary Material.

Dual-emission CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of mulberry leaves and sodium hydroxide. Briefly, dried and powdered mulberry leaves (0.25 g) and sodium hydroxide (0.50 g) were mixed with ultra-pure water (25 mL) in a Teflon-lined autoclave at 110 °C for 8 h. The obtained supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane, and dialyzed (500 D) against water for 24 h. After being filtered again (0.22 μm), dual-emission CDs were acquired by freeze-drying and stored at 4 °C for further use.

Determination of MP using bCDs was performed as follows. Firstly, MP (1.0 mg) was dissolved in sodium hydroxide solution (pH 12.0, 1.0 mL) and incubated at 70 °C for 25 min. Then, the hydrolyzed solution was added with HCl (pH 2.0, 1.0 mL) to reach pH 7.0. A series of above solution ranging from 0 to 40.0 μL was added into phosphate buffered saline (PBS) solution (pH 6.5, 10 mM, 1.0 mL) containing CDs (225.0 μg). After that, their fluorescent spectra in the range of 350–540 nm were collected with excitation wavelength at 320 nm.

For determination of glyphosate, PBS solution (pH 6.5, 10 mM, 1.0 mL), CDs (225.0 μg), and Cu2+ (9.0 μΜ) are pre-mixed and incubated at 37 °C for 60 min. Then, different amounts of glyphosate (6.0 mM, 0–23.0 μL) were added and incubated at 37 °C for 50 min. The fluorescent spectra in the range of 620–700 nm were recorded under excitation at 505 nm.

To evaluate the performance of CDs in practical applications, interferences were first introduced to investigate the selectivity. Selectivity of CDs to MP and glyphosate was evaluated by metal ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Al3+, Cd4+, Hg2+, 800.0 μM), amino acids (l-phenylalanine, l-Phe; l-Histidine, l-His; l-serine, l-Ser; l-leucine, l-Leu; l-glycine, l-Gly; l-proline, l-Pro; l-isoleucine, l-Lle; l-valine, l-Val; l-threonine, l-Thr; l-methionine, l-Met, 800.0 μM), and pesticides (glufosinate-ammonium, GA; quinalphos; dichlorvos; dimethoate; carbendazim; thiophanate-methyl, TM; 800.0 μM).

For real sample applications, food/herbal samples (i.e., apple, grape, cucumber, tomato, Fructus Piperis and Radix Angelicae) were selected. Samples were obtained from local supermarket or drugstore, Changsha, China. Real samples (1.0 kg) were sprayed with different concentrations of MP and glyphosate and dried at room temperature. Then the spiked samples were cut into pieces and ultrasonically extracted with ethanol:water (2000 mL, 1:1, v/v) for 30 min (Picó, Font, Ruiz, & Fernández, 2006). Thereafter, the extract was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane, concentrated to dryness under vacuum, redissolved with sodium hydroxide solution (pH 12.0, 1.0 mL), and incubated at 70 °C for 25 min. The hydrolyzed solution was neutralized by HCl (pH 2.0, 1.0 mL), and 0.1 mL of the solution was drawn and added into PBS solution (pH 6.5, 10 mM, 0.9 mL) containing CDs (225.0 μg) and Cu2+ (9.0 μM) (CDs-Cu2+ system was pre-mixed at 37 °C for 60 min). After incubation at 37 °C for 50 min, MP and glyphosate were determined by measuring the fluorescence intensities of bCDs and rCDs, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the dual-emission CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Natural biomass is considered as a renewable and inexpensive carbon and nitrogen source for CDs, while hydrothermal treatment of biomass always results in a single blue-emission CDs (Wareing, Gentile, & Phan, 2021). High levels of chlorophyll are always contained in biomass, which can be used as one of the starting materials to fabricate CDs with rich chlorophyll on the surface and red emission at about 660 nm (Zhao et al., 2017). However, chlorophyll is hydrophobic, which should be extracted by organic solvent (e.g., ethanol, acetone), and then mixed with polyoxyethylene bisamine to synthesize hydrophilic CDs using solvothermal method (Zhao et al., 2020). Hereof, a simple hydrothermal method was developed to prepare dual-emission CDs using mulberry leaves and sodium hydroxide as raw materials. Regarding the construction of dual-emission CDs with maximum sensitivity and high quality, reaction conditions were optimized (i.e., the amounts of mulberry leaves and sodium hydroxide, reaction temperature, and time) (Fig. S1). Obviously, fluorescence intensities of bCDs and rCDs reach the maximum when mulberry leaves (0.25 g) and sodium hydroxide (0.50 g) were mixed with pure water (25 mL) in a Teflon-lined autoclave for reaction at 110 °C for 8 h. Under optimization, quantum yields of bCDs and rCDs are 9.0 % (quinine sulfate as reference) and 6.1 % (rhodamine B as reference), respectively.

Various characterization methods were applied to confirm the successful synthesis of CDs. The surface morphology, particle size distribution, and internal crystal structure of CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM). As presented in Fig. 1 A, CDs exhibit uniform distribution with nearly spherical shape. In selected areas, the particle size ranges from 3 to 11 nm with an average particle size at 7 nm (Fig. S2A). Lattice fringes of CDs are clearly seen in high-resolution TEM image (inset in Fig. 1A), and the crystal plane spacing is 0.21 nm, which could be attributed to the characteristic (100) diffraction plane of the sp2 graphitic carbon (Zhu et al., 2022). X-ray diffraction (XRD) diagram of CDs in Fig. S2B displays an obvious wide diffraction peak at 2θ = 23°, suggesting the existence of graphite plane (002) of amorphous carbon structure (Tong et al., 2022). The XRD and TEM results exhibit the formation of graphene-like structures of CDs. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was carried out to identify the surface functional groups of CDs. As can be seen in Fig. 1B, absorption peaks of 3270, 2930, 1650, 1567, 1395 and 1034 cm−1 are assigned to OH/NH, CH, CO, CC, CN, and COC stretching vibrations. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results further confirmed the chemical compositions and surface functional groups of CDs. Full scan spectrum exhibits three characteristic peaks of C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s at the position of 285.1, 399.1, and 531.1 eV with relative elemental contents at 66.7 %, 6.0 %, and 27.3 % (Fig. 1C). High resolution XPS spectrum for C 1s is shown in Fig. S3A, peaks at 287.9, 286.3, 285.4, and 284.8 eV are owing to CN/CO, CO, CN, CC/CC. Fig. S3B exhibits high resolution XPS spectrum of N 1s, and the pyrrolic-N and graphitic-N/NH are found at 400.3 and 399.6 eV. While for high resolution XPS spectrum of O 1s, peaks at 535.78, 532.68, and 531.18 eV are contributed to OH, CO, and CO (Fig. S3C). The consistent results of XPS and FT-IR manifested the formation of abundant hydrophilic groups (e.g., –OH, –NH2, –COOH) on the surface of synthesized CDs.

The optical performance of CDs is demonstrated in Fig. 1D. UV–vis spectrum shows that CDs have three characteristic absorption peaks (i.e., 275, 405, and 650 nm). Absorption peak at 275 nm could be ascribed to π–π* transition of CN, CO, or CC in the nanocarbon. The other two peaks located at 405 nm and 650 nm might be related to n–π* transition of CO and CN bonds in chlorophyll (Zhao et al., 2017). In fluorescence measurement, CDs display dual fluorescent emission wavelengths at 440 and 660 nm under 320 nm excitation. bCDs show the maximum excitation peak at 320 nm, while rCDs obviously exhibit three excitation peaks at 405, 505, and 600 nm. For bCDs, with the increase of excitation wavelengths from 280 nm to 340 nm, the emission wavelengths show redshift from 434 nm to 444 nm, and the fluorescence intensity increases gradually, reaches the maximum at 320 nm excitation, and then decreases (Fig. S4). Hence, excitation-dependent behavior is observed for bCDs, which might derive from internal carbon core with different particle sizes and complicated surface states (Tong et al., 2021). With the increase of excitation wavelengths from 465 nm to 525 nm, the emission wavelength of rCDs unchanges, and the fluorescence intensity shows an obvious change with a maximum value at 505 nm excitation (Fig. S4). Thus, excitation-independent behavior is presented for rCDs, probably coming from the surface chlorophyll. Consequently, as-prepared dual-emission CDs are derived from internal CDs and surface chlorophyll.

Optical stability of CDs was oneoftheveryimportantfactors in sensing, thus effects of ionic strength, storagetime, and light irradiation time were investigated. As exhibited in Fig S5, fluorescent intensities at 440 and 660 nm for bCDs and rCDs have negligible change in NaCl solution from 0 to 800 mM, at 4 °C storage in PBS solution for 10 weeks, and under 365 nm irradiation for 120 min. Hence, the obtained CDs possessed outstanding optical stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of simultaneously sensing parathion-methyl and glyphosate
ce:section.ce:para: Development of fluorescent methods to distinguish different targets have attracted widespread attentions. However, to our best knowledge, discrimination and simultaneous detection of two pesticides by CDs were rarely mentioned. Scheme 1 is a representative diagram of fluorescent sensing strategy for MP and glyphosate. Specifically, PNP, the hydrolyzed product of MP, quenches the fluorescence of bCDs (Fig. S6A), thus fluorescence intensity of bCDs at 440 nm can be relevant to MP content. Glyphosate and chlorophyll competitively complex with Cu2+ to change the fluorescence of rCDs at 660 nm for glyphosate detection. Significantly, choosing appropriate excitation wavelengths is very crucial for eliminating crosstalk interference and realizing simultaneous detection. When the excitation wavelength was set at 320 nm, fluorescence of bCDs at 440 nm can be quenched by PNP, but cannot be affected by Cu2+ (Fig. S6A). And at the same time, when the excitation wavelength was set at 505 nm (greater than the absorption band of PNP, 482 nm), fluorescence of rCDs at 660 nm obviously response to Cu2+ and glyphosate without the influence of MP assay system (Fig. S6A). As a result, 320 nm and 505 nm were chosen as appropriate excitation wavelengths for simultaneous detection of MP and by bCDs (Em 440 nm) and rCDs (660 nm).

After alkaline hydrolysis of MP at pH 12, solution color turns from colorless to yellow, and the maximum absorption peak redshifts from 271 to 402 nm (Fig. S6B), suggesting the generation of PNP. In addition, high-resolution MS of hydrolyzed product in positive ion mode indicates a diagnostic ion at m/z 140.0407 (C6H5NO3, 124 Da less than that of MP) (Fig. S6C and D), further indicating the successful production of PNP. The pivotal factors (pH, reaction temperature, and time) were investigated to ensure a relatively high hydrolysis efficiency of MP. After optimization, pH 12, 70 °C, and 25 min were selected (Fig. S7), and hydrolysis efficiency reaches 87.7 %. In PBS solution (pH 6.5, 10 mM), the absorption spectrum of PNP presents two typical peaks at 320 and 400 nm, which has a large overlap with the excitation and emission spectra of bCDs (Fig. 2 A). Thus, it can be deduced that IFE happens after adding PNP into bCDs. In Fig. 2B, the average fluorescence lifetime of bCDs is fixed at 5.09 ns whether PNP is present or not, which implies that the quenching phenomenon is just a simple light re-absorption process, and fluorescence resonance energy transfer should be ruled out (Tong et al., 2020).

Surface chlorophyll can selectively coordinate with Cu2+, and the new formed non-fluorescent complex causes the fluorescence quenching of rCDs (Fig. 2C). In this situation, formation of more stable glyphosate-Cu2+ complex will recover the fluorescence of rCDs (Fig. 2C). Thus, an off–on mode to sense glyphosate was established using Cu2+ as an intermediate. UV–vis absorption peak of CDs at 660 nm blueshifts to 625 nm after addition of Cu2+, which is back to 660 nm by the following addition of glyphosate (Fig. S8A). It can be concluded that non-fluorescent ground-state complex of CDs-Cu2+ might be formed. The conclusion could be confirmed by the unchanged fluorescence lifetime of rCDs (5.13 ns) (Fig. 2D). Besides, Stern-Volmer equation was applied to elucidate the process. As illustrated in Fig. S8B, F0-F/F0 at 660 nm shows excellent linear correlation with the concentration of Cu2+. The quenching rate constants (Kq ) are calculated as 3.5 × 1012, 7.2 × 1012, and 1.1 × 1013 M−1S−1 at 10, 20, and 30 °C, which exhibits an increasing tendency with the increment of temperature. This manifests that forming CDs-Cu2+ complex is an endothermic process. The Kq values are larger than 1.0 × 1010 M−1S−1, which indicates that the quenching mechanism is SQE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization and detection of parathion-methyl and glyphosate
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve better response for MP and glyphosate, main detection parameters, including CDs concentration, pH, and reaction temperature were evaluated (Fig. S9). Results indicate that when CDs concentration, pH, and reaction temperature are set at 225 μg/mL, 6.5, and 37 °C, the fluorescent quenching efficiency reaches the highest. Reaction equilibrium arrives after mixing CDs with PNP for 1 min (Fig. S9G). Fluorescence intensity of bCDs at 440 nm declines gradually with the increase of PNP, which exhibits good linear range from 0.3 to 65.0 μM (Fig. S10). Consequently, it is plausible to detect MP using IFE between bCDs and PNP. As depicted in Fig. 3 A, fluorescence intensity of bCDs is quenched gradually with the increase of MP concentration, and a superior linear relationship is observed in the range of 0.3–65.0 μM (y = 0.0090 × – 0.0016, R2 = 0.9988). The limit of detection (LOD) of bCDs to MP is calculated as 0.14 μM (3σ/S, σ and S are the standard deviation of blank samples and the slope of linear equation, n = 12).

For glyphosate detection with higher sensitivity, reaction parameters for rCDs-Cu2+ system were first evaluated and optimized. Fig. S9H exhibits that stable rCDs-Cu2+ complex can be formed after 60 min incubation with pH at 6.5 and temperature at 37 °C. The fluorescence intensity of rCDs at 660 nm decreases with the increasing concentration of Cu2+, and a good linearity is presented in the range of 0–9.0 μM (y = 0.0983x + 0.0218, R2 = 0.9950) (Fig. S11). When the Cu2+ concentration is set at 9.0 μM, a maximum quenching efficiency (90.7 %) is found. Subsequently, the quenched fluorescence of rCDs-Cu2+ system remarkably recovers by introduction of glyphosate within 50 min (Fig. S12). Under the optimized conditions, the fluorescence recovers gradually with the addition of glyphosate, and a good linear relationship is found in the range of 1.0–110.0 μM (y = 0.0551 × + 0.3390, R2 = 0.9973) (Fig. 3). LOD for glyphosate detection is 0.60 μM (3σ/S).

In order to realize simultaneous detection, CDs should be first incubated with Cu2+ at 37 °C for 60 min in PBS solution (pH 6.5, 10 mM). At the same time, sample including MP and glyphosate was hydrolyzed in alkaline solution (pH 12) at 70 °C for 25 min and neutralized. After that, sample and CDs-Cu2+ solution were mixed and incubated for 50 min. Finally, dual-channel strategy was established for simultaneous detection, i.e., Ex at 320 nm and Em at 440 nm for MP, Ex at 505 nm and Em at 660 nm for glyphosate. As showcased in Fig. 4 , MP and glyphosate mixtures with different concentrations can be simultaneously detected in the same reaction system because of the independent two-channel detection and nonexistent crosstalk interference. Table 1 presents the developed fluorescent sensors for MP and glyphosate detection. Obviously, almost all of them focused on the single-target detection. Hence, our strategy contains outstanding performance, such as simultaneous detection, appropriate linear range, and high sensitivity.

Reproducibility was also evaluated by detection of MP and glyphosate. The relative standard deviation (RSD) values are 0.28–0.55 % and 1.34–1.69 % for intra-day and inter-day variations, which exhibits the excellent precision.

Anti-interference and selectivity are especially important for real sample assays. Then a number of possible interferences in agricultural samples, such as metal ions, amino acids, pesticides were selected and tested (Figs. S13 and S14). Obviously, they have negligible interference for MP and glyphosate detection.

Four kinds of food (i.e., apple, grape, cucumber, and tomato), and two kinds of herbs (i.e., Fructus Piperis and Radix Angelicae) were selected to evaluate the application of the developed method. Different amounts of MP and glyphosate were spiked into herbs, and Table S1 displays the detection results, which is in agreement with those detected by traditional HPLC/HPLC–MS method. As expected, MP and glyphosate are not detected in herbs. Good recoveries are observed for MP from 91.01 % to 105.27 % and for glyphosate from 90.93 % to 108.50 %. And the RSDs are less than 8.47 % and 8.43 % for MP and glyphosate. Results demonstrate that the as-prepared dual-emission CDs can realize dual pesticides determination with excellent performance in real samples.

In the field of information technology, CDs-based logic gates have gained more attentions and contributed to many captivating research (Tong et al., 2021). Enlightened by the excellent fluorescent sensing performance of the dual-emission CDs, it is combined with logic gate to access environment, food, and herbal safety (Fig. 4B). The residual OPs in food/herbs should not be over 20 μg/kg according to Pharmacopoeia of People's Republic of China (Chinese Pharmacopoeia Committee, 2020). For MP, the threshold value of quenching efficiency of bCDs is calculated to be 6.68 %. “0″ (<) and “1” (>) are defined as permitted and excessive concentrations in real samples. For glyphosate, the recovered efficiency of rCDs-Cu2+ system is 99.08 % as threshold value. The permitted (<) and excessive (>) amounts of glyphosate are represented as “0” and “1”. The table of this logic gate is shown in Fig. 5B. When the input is (0,0), it indicates the amounts of MP and glyphosate both meet the criterion (<20 μg/kg), and the output is “0”. While output “1” denotes that either or both of them exceeds the standard (≥20 μg/kg), and the corresponding input signal can be (1,0), (0,1), or (1,1). Therefore, the established dual-emission CDs system can be successfully applied in OR gate for semi-quantification of MP and glyphosate. And it can be employed in portable devices to ensure environment, food, and herbal safety.

In summary, for the first time, a nonenzymatic fluorescent sensing platform based on dual-emission CDs was developed for discriminating and simultaneously detecting MP and glyphosate. The strategy was achieved by the IFE and SQE between MP/glyphosate and bCDs/rCDs. This study not only introduces a simple hydrothermal method for synthesizing dual-emission CDs, but also proposes a facile, sensitive, efficient, and selective nonenzymatic strategy for simultaneously detecting MP and glyphosate in real complex samples. The proposed strategy opens a new paradigm for multi-pesticides detection. Moreover, the design and application of dual-emission CDs can also be extended as promising tools to detect multianalytes in food, environment, and health field.

Yangyu Yang: Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Xia Tong: Investigation, Validation, Data curation, Formal analysis. Yuxia Chen: Data curation, Validation, Visualization, Conceptualization. Rongrong Zhou: Investigation, Validation. Guihan Cai: Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Tongtao Wang: Data curation, Validation. Shuihan Zhang: Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Shuyun Shi: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Ying Guo: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the Key Research and Development projects in Hunan Province, China (2021NK2024), the Key Research Program of Hunan Administration of Traditional Chinese Medicine (C2022042), and the Special Project of “National Chinese Materia Medica (CMM) Resources Survey” ([2017] 66).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2022.134346.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
